settings.body:
Every anti-ageing product on the shelf promises one thing: more collagen. Retinoids stimulate it. Peptides mimic fragments of it. Lasers and microneedling injure the skin just enough to trigger its synthesis. It's the single protein most associated with "young-looking" skin — and also the first casualty of ageing, dropping roughly 1% a year after age 20.

Red light therapy has a claim in this space that's fundamentally different from topicals or trauma-based treatments: it stimulates collagen photochemically, without damaging the skin surface. Here's exactly how that works at the cellular level.

Collagen isn't produced in your outer skin layer (the epidermis). It's synthesised in the dermis — the deeper layer — by specialised cells called fibroblasts. A healthy young fibroblast can produce several thousand collagen fibrils per day. Fibroblasts in older, sun-damaged, or stressed skin produce far fewer, and the collagen they do produce is often disorganised.

Any intervention that boosts collagen must reach the dermis, reach fibroblasts, and trigger the molecular pathway that drives synthesis. 660nm red light does exactly this.

When 660nm photons enter the dermis, they're absorbed primarily by cytochrome c oxidase — the terminal enzyme in the mitochondrial electron transport chain. This absorption event triggers a cascade of measurable changes in exposed fibroblasts within minutes:

Step 1 — mitochondrial ATP production increases by 25–40% within the first hour, providing the energy currency fibroblasts need to synthesise new protein.
Step 2 — reactive oxygen species (ROS) drop, reducing the oxidative pressure that inhibits fibroblast activity in aged skin.
Step 3 — the transcription factors AP-1 and NF-κB upregulate, which in turn activates genes encoding Type I and Type III procollagen.
Step 4 — over the following 48–72 hours, new collagen fibrils are assembled, cross-linked, and deposited into the extracellular matrix.

settings.label: Average increase in dermal collagen density after 12 weeks of 660nm red light therapy in split-face RCTs.
settings.citation: Barolet et al., Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B, 2021

settings.quote: Unlike ablative lasers or microneedling, red light achieves collagen stimulation without triggering the inflammatory cascade of wound healing — reducing downtime to zero.

settings.text: What this looks like in the mirror

settings.body:
Collagen synthesis is slow. A single session doesn't move the needle visibly — but 4–5 sessions per week over 8–12 weeks consistently produces measurable outcomes in controlled trials: reduced fine-line depth, improved elasticity, more even skin tone, and a subjective "glow" that participants rate highly in blinded assessments.

The effect compounds. Studies that followed users beyond 24 weeks showed continued improvement with continued use, suggesting the dermal remodelling is cumulative rather than a short-term rebound.

settings.title: The Lumisca Glow protocol
settings.body: 10 minutes per session, 4–5 sessions per week. Combined 660nm and 850nm wavelengths cover both superficial fibroblast activation and deeper dermal repair. Most users report visible skin-tone improvement by week 4 and texture change by week 8 — timings consistent with the cellular cascade described above.

The reason red light therapy has moved from fringe to mainstream in dermatology is simple: the pathway is mapped. We know which photons are absorbed by which enzymes to trigger which genes, and we can measure the result — more collagen, better-organised collagen, and visibly different skin. It's not magic. It's just biochemistry, catalysed by a very specific wavelength of light.

settings.heading: How red light triggers collagen production at the cellular level
settings.deck: Inside the fibroblast pathway that drives every visible improvement.
